Social Digital Places include social media platforms, online forums, and other digital venues that enable users to connect, share, and interact in a social context. This class is of significant cultural relevance, not only due to its frequent use by cultural actors, but also due to the growing tendency to use these spaces as venues for digital exhibitions. Example includes Museo del Prado’s TikTok or exhibitions curated and displayed in Instagram.

This class refers to a semantic, intellectual, or conceptual element that articulates specific ideas. It can take the form of assertions, theses, hypotheses, arguments, interpretations, among others. Propositions are inherently part of "propositional entities." They can be developed through “conceptual works”, conveyed and embodied in propositional objects, and assigned to any entity.

modelingNote.value: Proposition is propositional content. It is related to conceptual objects  via conveys and for propositional objects via embodies.

description.en: The "Destruction" class represents the process and act of causing material objects to become damaged, dismantled, or rendered unusable. This includes any activities that lead to the degradation, disintegration, or obliteration of objects, whether intentional or accidental. The Destruction class is crucial for documenting and understanding the loss or alteration of material objects within various contexts, such as art conservation, historical documentation, and cultural heritage preservation.

Represents a specific segment or part of a larger spatial structure that serves a distinct function or holds significance within the overall environment. This class includes rooms, halls, wings, or any other designated areas within buildings, complexes, or other types of larger spaces. For example: the “Sala Noble” (spatial unit) is the spatial unit of Palacio de Villalón (Building – physical site), which is the headquarters of Museo Carmen Thyssen (Museum – cultural institution).

The "Open Day" class represents an event where an institution, such as a museum, gallery, or cultural organization, opens its doors to the public, often free of charge, to provide access to exhibitions, facilities, and special activities. Open Days are designed to engage the community, attract new visitors, and provide educational and recreational opportunities. These events typically include guided tours, workshops, performances, and interactive sessions to enhance the visitor experience.
A fundamental unit that represents an abstract idea, entity, or category that encapsulates the essential characteristics and properties of an object, event, or phenomenon.
This class represents physical sites.

Propositional Activity designates a planned activity whose defining component includes one or more propositions articulated as its propositional content. It covers activities in which statements, arguments, interpretations, or discursive framings are central to what the activity is and does, enabling them to be related to "Proposition" via "conveys" within OntoExhibit.

Curatorial Proposal designates a propositional entity that sets out a curatorial project in a preliminary, projective form. It articulates the intended concept, scope, rationale, and curatorial approach—often including aims, selection principles, thematic lines, key references, and practical constraints—so that the project can be discussed, evaluated, negotiated, or commissioned prior to its realization.

Embodies relates a propositional object to a proposition when that proposition is materially instantiated in the object (physically or computationally), i.e., realized in its structure, configuration, or operation.

The "hasDigitalAnalogous" property represents a relationship between an entity and its exact digital reproduction. This property is used to link entities to their precise digital counterparts, which maintain all attributes and characteristics of the original. Examples of such digital analogues include a PDF version of a book, a digital twin of a building or city, or an exact 3D reconstruction of a physical object. The "hasDigitalAnalogous" property emphasizes the fidelity and accuracy of the digital reproduction in relation to the original entity, ensuring that the digital version tends to serve as an exact representation.

Embodies relates a propositional object to a proposition when that proposition is materially instantiated in the object (physically or computationally), i.e., realized in its structure, configuration, or operation.

This property relates an Activity (including both Process and Planned_Activity) to its broader place of occurrence—the territorial or techno-spatial context in which the activity unfolds. It is used to record the general geographic or infrastructural setting of an activity (e.g., a city, region, country, or a digital/technical environment), independently of the specific host site. In this model, takesPlaceAt complements hasVenue: while hasVenue points to the concrete hosting site (e.g., a museum building, gallery, institutional facility, or platform-as-site), takesPlaceAt captures the wider territorial or techno-place context that situates that venue and the activity within a larger spatial frame.

Typical use:

Exhibition hasVenue Villanueva building (Museo del Prado) (site)

Exhibition takesPlaceAt Madrid (territorial entity)

Online activities may use takesPlaceAt to indicate the relevant techno-place (e.g., a platform/ecosystem) when the broader infrastructural context matters.

This property identifies the agents (such as institutions, gallerists, collectors, etc.) that participate in an exhibition with their works. This property links an exhibition or event to the exhibitors who contribute by providing or showcasing their collections, acknowledging their role in the exhibition. For instance: Galería Alfredo Viñas isExhibitorIn Arco 2020 (art fair).
The official name or designation of an entity.

This property relates an Activity (including both Process and Planned_Activity) to its broader place of occurrence—the territorial or techno-spatial context in which the activity unfolds. It is used to record the general geographic or infrastructural setting of an activity (e.g., a city, region, country, or a digital/technical environment), independently of the specific host site. In this model, takesPlaceAt complements hasVenue: while hasVenue points to the concrete hosting site (e.g., a museum building, gallery, institutional facility, or platform-as-site), takesPlaceAt captures the wider territorial or techno-place context that situates that venue and the activity within a larger spatial frame.

Typical use:

Exhibition hasVenue Villanueva building (Museo del Prado) (site)

Exhibition takesPlaceAt Madrid (territorial entity)

Online activities may use takesPlaceAt to indicate the relevant techno-place (e.g., a platform/ecosystem) when the broader infrastructural context matters.
A detailed list of the technical specifications and requirements of the techno-actant.

It is used to record the general geographic or infrastructural setting of an activity (e.g., a city, region, country, or a digital/technical environment), independently of the specific host site. In this model, takesPlaceAt complements hasVenue: while hasVenue points to the concrete hosting site (e.g., a museum building, gallery, institutional facility, or platform-as-site), takesPlaceAt captures the wider territorial or techno-place context that situates that venue and the activity within a larger spatial frame.

Exhibition takesPlaceAt Madrid (territorial entity)

Online activities may use takesPlaceAt to indicate the relevant techno-place (e.g., a platform/ecosystem) when the broader infrastructural context matters.

It is used to record the general geographic or infrastructural setting of an activity (e.g., a city, region, country, or a digital/technical environment), independently of the specific host site. In this model, takesPlaceAt complements hasVenue: while hasVenue points to the concrete hosting site (e.g., a museum building, gallery, institutional facility, or platform-as-site), takesPlaceAt captures the wider territorial or techno-place context that situates that venue and the activity within a larger spatial frame.

Exhibition takesPlaceAt Madrid (territorial entity)

Online activities may use takesPlaceAt to indicate the relevant techno-place (e.g., a platform/ecosystem) when the broader infrastructural context matters.
A detailed list of the technical specifications and requirements of the techno-actant.

The "identifier" data property represents a unique code, number, or string assigned to an entity within various authority vocabularies. This property is used to specify the authoritative identifier that distinguishes an entity in controlled vocabularies, databases, or registries maintained by recognized institutions or organizations. Identifiers ensure consistent and unambiguous reference to entities across different systems and contexts.
Indicates that a human agent holds a membership within a collective human agent as represented by the Membership class.
Describes the prizes or recognitions that will be awarded to the winners.

This property identifies the creative agents who participate in an exhibition by showcasing their works. This property links an exhibition or event to the agents (individuals or groups) responsible for exhibiting their creations, thereby acknowledging their role in the presentation of the artworks. For instance: Picasso is ExhibitingActantIn the exhibition “Picasso, 1906. The great transformation".
Number of inhabitants.
Conveys relates a propositional entity to one or more propositions that it bears as propositional content.

This property identifies the agents (such as institutions, gallerists, collectors, etc.) that participate in an exhibition with their works. This property links an exhibition or event to the exhibitors who contribute by providing or showcasing their collections, acknowledging their role in the exhibition. For instance: Galería Alfredo Viñas isExhibitorIn Arco 2020 (art fair).
A detailed list of the technical specifications and requirements of the techno-actant.

The "hasExhibitingActant" property identifies the creative agents who participate in an exhibition by showcasing their works. This property links an exhibition or event to the agents (individuals or groups) responsible for exhibiting their creations, thereby acknowledging their role in the presentation of the artworks. For instance: Picasso is ExhibitingActantIn the exhibition “Picasso, 1906. The great transformation.

This property relates an Event to a spatial anchor—physical or digital—used to situate the event in space when such contextualization is meaningful. It is intentionally broad and optional: many events can be spatially located in principle, but the location may be unknown, irrelevant, or not recorded. Use hasSpatialLocus for lightweight contextual placement (e.g., an interaction occurring in a messaging platform, an assignment made within an institutional system, a reaction recorded via a device), without implying that the event has a formal venue or that spatial information is a primary descriptive feature.

Typical use: contextual, “if needed” spatialization of any event (including non-programmed or non-operational events).
A detailed textual description of the entity, including its purpose, functionalities, and key features.

The "hasDigitalVersion" property represents a relationship between an entity and its digital counterpart, where the digital version is not merely a reproduction but a variation or interpretation of the original entity. This digital version may incorporate additional elements, contextual adjustments, or creative reinterpretations that distinguish it from a straightforward digital copy. The property emphasizes the transformative nature of the digital version, which can offer new perspectives, enhanced features, or alternative forms of engagement with the original entity. The relationship between an entity and its exact digital counterpart is defined as “hasDigitalAnalogous,” while the relationship between an activity and its mere recording is defined as “hasDigitalRecording.”

The "appellation" data property represents alternative names, titles, or designations assigned to an entity, other than the official name. This property is used to specify commonly recognized names, nicknames, colloquial titles, or other forms of identification that serve to distinguish and identify the entity beyond its official designation. Instances are El Greco, Museo Reina Sofía (being the official name: Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía), Big Apple (New York), El Quijote, etc.

Relates an organization schema to the ordering principle(s) it implements. A schema may implement multiple principles (e.g., thematic + chronological), and a principle may be realized by multiple schemas.
